title: Customer Service Device Authorization Hijack via Social Engineering
narrative: Fraudster calls customer support claiming device access issues, convincing agent to whitelist a new device fingerprint. Within 90 minutes of the service call, transactions emerge from the newly-authorized device showing geographic inconsistency: original account shows 47 transactions from addr1=Chicago over 8 months, new device immediately transacts from addr1=Miami with dist1>1100km. The fraudster rapidly escalates purchase amounts: first transaction $89 (testing), second $347, then three transactions of $899-$1240 within 4 hours, all targeting high-resale ProductCD='W' electronics. Card velocity spikes from historical 2.1 transactions/day to 8 transactions in 6 hours. The new device shows DeviceInfo string never previously associated with this card1, and P_emaildomain switches from original gmail.com to a newly-added protonmail.com alias. CVV and AVS match perfectly because fraudster obtained full card details during account compromise phase before the service call.
indicators: New DeviceInfo string appears within 2 hours of customer service interaction timestamp
Geographic jump: dist1 exceeds 800km between last legitimate transaction and first new-device transaction
Transaction velocity: card1_txn_count_24hr jumps from baseline 0-3 to 6+ transactions
Amount escalation pattern: first transaction <$100, subsequent transactions 3-12x larger within 4-hour window
P_emaildomain change to privacy-focused provider (protonmail, tutanota) within 48 hours of new device authorization
ProductCD concentration: 75%+ of new-device transactions target 'W' (electronics) or 'C' (high-value categories)
Perfect CVV/AVS match despite sudden behavioral deviation indicating full account credential access
summary: A new device begins transacting on an account within hours of a customer service call, originating from a location over 800km away from the account's historical activity, with transaction amounts rapidly escalating from under $100 to over $1000 within hours.
reasoning: The fraudster socially engineers support staff to whitelist their device, then exploits full account access to rapidly extract value before detection, targeting high-resale electronics while the authorization window remains open.
indicator_explanations: Brand new device shows up right after the cardholder called support
Card suddenly used hundreds of miles from its normal location
Daily transaction count spikes dramatically above the cardholder's usual pattern
Purchases start small then rapidly escalate to much larger amounts
Email address switched to a secure provider often used for anonymity
Fraudster focuses overwhelmingly on high-value electronics or premium goods
All security checks pass cleanly despite completely abnormal purchasing behavior
